original:
A CHRISTMAS CAROL

A Christmas Carol in Prose, Being a Ghost Story of Christmas by Charles Dickens

PREFACE

In this spooky little book, I've tried to conjure up an idea that won't make my readers upset with themselves, each other, the holiday season, or me
simplified: A Christmas Carol is a ghost Christmas story by Charles Dickens.

original: I hope it haunts their homes in a good way, and nobody wants to get rid of it
simplified: I made this book to not make people feel bad.

original: Your faithful friend and servant, C
simplified: I hope it makes their homes happy.

original: December, 1843
simplified: Your friend, C.

original:
CONTENTS

Stave I: Marley's Ghost
Stave II: The First of the Three Spirits
Stave III: The Second of the Three Spirits
Stave IV: The Last of the Spirits
Stave V: The End of It

STAVE I: MARLEY'S GHOST

Marley was dead: to begin with
simplified: December, 1843

original: There's absolutely no doubt about that
simplified: Part 1: Marley's Ghost

original: The record of his burial was signed by the clergyman, the clerk, the undertaker, and the chief mourner
simplified: Part 2: The First Spirit

original: Scrooge signed it, and his signature was respected in the business world for anything he chose to invest in
simplified: Part 3: The Second Spirit

original: Old Marley was as dead as a doornail
simplified: Part 4: The Last Spirit

original: Remember, I'm not saying that I personally know why a doornail is considered particularly dead
simplified: Part 5: The End

original: I might have thought a coffin-nail was the deadest piece of iron in the trade
simplified: A nail for a coffin is very dead.

original: But the wisdom of our ancestors is in that comparison; and I won't argue with it, or we're all in trouble
simplified: But old wisdom says it is true. I will not fight it.

original: So, I'll emphasize again that Marley was as dead as a doornail
simplified: So, Marley was dead as a nail.

original: Scrooge knew he was dead, right
simplified: Scrooge knew Marley was dead.

original: Of course, he did
simplified: Yes, he did.

original: How could it be otherwise
simplified: It could not be different.

original: Scrooge and he had been partners for I don't know how many years
simplified: Scrooge and Marley worked together for many years.

original: Scrooge was his sole executor, his sole administrator, his only heir, his only friend, and his only mourner
simplified: Scrooge got everything from Marley.

original: And yet, Scrooge wasn't so terribly affected by the sad event that he didn't make a great business deal on the day of the funeral and celebrated it with a definite bargain
simplified: But Scrooge was not very sad. He made a good business deal on the funeral day.

original: Bringing up Marley's funeral reminds me to make it clear that Marley was definitely dead
simplified: Talking about the funeral, Marley was really dead.

original: This must be understood, or nothing wonderful can come from the story I'm about to tell
simplified: You need to know this to enjoy the story.

original: If we weren't completely sure that Hamlet's Father was dead before the play started, him strolling at night in an easterly wind on his own castle walls wouldn't be any more remarkable than any other middle-aged man foolishly going out after dark in a windy place—like Saint Paul's Churchyard, for instance—just to surprise his son
simplified: If we don't know Hamlet's Father is dead, seeing him is not special.

original: Scrooge never removed Old Marley's name from the business sign
simplified: Scrooge keeps Old Marley's name on the sign.

original: Years later, it still read "Scrooge and Marley" above the warehouse door
simplified: The sign still says "Scrooge and Marley."

original: The firm was known as Scrooge and Marley
simplified: The business is called Scrooge and Marley.

original: Sometimes people new to the business called him Scrooge, and sometimes Marley, but he answered to both
simplified: People call him Scrooge or Marley. He does not mind.

original: It was all the same to him
simplified: It does not matter to him.

original: Oh, but he was a tight-fisted hand at the grindstone, Scrooge
simplified: Scrooge works very hard for his money.

original: A squeezing, wrenching, grasping, scraping, clutching, greedy old sinner
simplified: He is a very greedy man.

original: Hard and sharp as flint, from which no steel had ever struck out a generous spark; secretive, and self-contained, and as isolated as an oyster
simplified: He is cold and alone like an oyster.

original: The cold within him froze his old features, nipped his pointed nose, shriveled his cheek, stiffened his walk; made his eyes red, his thin lips blue, and his voice was sharp and grating
simplified: Scrooge was very cold and his face looked old.

original: His head and eyebrows were frosty, and his chin was bristly
simplified: His hair was white and his chin was rough.

original: He carried his own low temperature around with him; he chilled his office in the dog days of summer and didn't warm it one degree at Christmas
simplified: Scrooge was always cold; he made his office cold even in summer and did not make it warm for Christmas.

original: External heat and cold had little influence on Scrooge
simplified: Hot or cold weather did not change Scrooge.

original: No warmth could warm him, no cold weather chill him
simplified: No heat could make him warm, no cold could make him cold.

original: No wind that blew was more bitter than he, no falling snow was more determined, no pelting rain less likely to be persuaded
simplified: Scrooge was colder and more set than any wind, snow, or rain.

original: Bad weather didn't know what to do with him
simplified: Bad weather did not affect him.

original: The heaviest rain, snow, hail, and sleet could only boast of one advantage over him—they often came down generously, and Scrooge never did
simplified: Rain, snow, hail, and sleet were more giving than Scrooge.

original: Nobody ever stopped him on the street to say, with a cheerful look, "My dear Scrooge, how are you
simplified: No one ever stopped Scrooge to say, "My dear Scrooge, how are you?"

original: When will you come to see me
simplified: "When will you come to see me?"
